summary: pywingui ships hardcoded phone-home and remote kill-switch in licensing module
details: pywingui/licensing/client.py hardcodes _API_URL = "https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbx8av4kWAmq_LkN2kafG8fKTc6k52orDbz-hsw9j2PD0eEl7mQtnNOXjVpSWRoPuCXs/exec" and pywingui/licensing/manager.py.ensure_valid() POSTs to that author-controlled Google Apps Script with a persistent seat_id, the package name, and the installer's project/app name (auto-derived from Settings().DEFAULT_APP_TITLE_RE) starting on day 31 of use. If the backend does not return allowed=True, manager.py raises RuntimeError, hard-stopping the installer's automation. There is no user opt-in, no documented disclosure, and no offline fallback. The README explicitly advertises "No Execution on Import", "Zero-PII", "100% local", and "no hidden pings or background telemetry" — claims that directly contradict the shipped code. The author retains a unilateral remote kill-switch over every installed copy: changing the Apps Script response can brick all downstream automation. The bulk of the public API ships as Nuitka-compiled.pyd binaries (controller, app/context, base/base_window, ~40 files), so the exact invocation boundary (import vs first connect) is not externally auditable, but the licensing chokepoint and its destination are clearly visible in the plain-Python wrapper. This is silent-relay of installer-side application identifiers to an author endpoint plus a remote denial-of-service primitive over the consumer's pipeline.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
